forecasts.reasoning: Historically, changes to the Dow Jones Industrial Average are infrequent, typically occurring every 1 to 3 years. The index recently underwent changes in February 2024 (Amazon replacing Walgreens) and November 2024 (Nvidia replacing Intel, Sherwin-Williams replacing Dow Inc). These consecutive recent changes make another removal within the next 2-3 months extremely unlikely, as the committee prefers to maintain index continuity unless forced by significant corporate events (spinoffs, mergers, or severely diminished stock prices). Thorough searches of recent news, financial reporting (WSJ, CNBC, Bloomberg, Reuters), and official S&P Dow Jones announcements from early 2025 confirm no current rumors, announcements, or credible reports of impending removals for any of the 30 component companies before July 1, 2025. Given the short timeframe of ~2 months remaining until the resolution date and the absence of any driving catalysts (like pending mega-mergers among constituents), the probability is practically at the floor. In accordance with constraints requiring probabilities between 0.05 and 0.95, I am setting the estimate at 0.05.

The possibility of Harvard University losing its 501(c)(3) tax-exempt status by July 1, 2025 (in slightly over two months) is virtually nonexistent. Although the Trump administration has strongly threatened this and reportedly requested the IRS to take action against Harvard's tax status as of mid-April 2025, standard IRS revocation procedures involve a lengthy process of audits, notices, and administrative appeals that universally span years, not weeks. Furthermore, a 1998 federal law prohibits the president from directing the IRS to target specific taxpayers. Even if the administration attempts an expedited and unlawful process, Harvard—the wealthiest university in the U.S. with formidable legal resources—would instantly seek and almost certainly obtain preliminary injunctions or temporary restraining orders in federal court, delaying any official loss of status until well past July 2025. Given the legal hurdles, procedural requirements, and inevitable extreme litigation, an incredibly short timeline of just over two months makes this outcome effectively impossible.

Extensive searches indicate that no successful coup has taken place in Africa or Latin America between September 2023 and the knowledge cutoff of April 25, 2025. Therefore, the question relies entirely on the likelihood of a successful coup occurring in the 66 days separating the knowledge cutoff and the July 1, 2025 resolution date. 

Although Africa experienced a wave of coups between 2020 and 2023 (averaging roughly 1.5 to 2 per year), the complete absence of any successful coup over the past 20 months suggests the immediate contagion phase has abated. If we assume a forward-looking base rate of 0.5 to 1 successful coups per year, the Poisson probability of at least one successful event transpiring in an 0.18-year (66 day) window is between 9% and 16%. 

Additionally, specific near-term risks in the region appear muted; the ruling junta in Gabon decisively won the April 2025 election, avoiding a potential "self-coup" or counter-coup flashpoint, and there are no immediate high-risk electoral crises apparent in late April through June 2025. Given the short time horizon and the recent cooling of the coup trend, I estimate the probability rests at a conservative 12%.
